metadata.activity_description:
Activity:  Open and Navigate Endstar

Students must be able to open Endstar, navigate all functions of Endstar and be prepared to answer questions about the platform as part of this introductory activity.

This activity is a basic ‘get to know’ the Endstar programme.

Criteria: We know we are successful when….

✔ Log in successfully to Endstar

✔ Navigate the basic interface (toolbar, camera, workspace)

✔ Upload one screenshot of their environment to their Portfolio

✔ Identify the purpose of key tools (move, tile, prop, erase, inspect)

✔ Adjust essential settings for smoother performance (resolution, fullscreen, mouse sensitivity)

✔ Use AI to ask at least one productive question about the platform (e.g., “What does this tool do?”)

✔ Explain how Endstar’s UI compares to other platforms they’ve used

✔ Discover advanced properties (scale, rotation, object attributes)

✔ Use AI thoughtfully—evaluating whether its suggestions are accurate, useful, or biased

Teaching note: Before starting any work please have the students complete the diagnostic here. They will repeat the same set of questions in lesson 24.

Teaching Note: Optional Activity. The Studio can be accessed by the students outside of the classroom.

Teaching Note: Optional Activity. The Studio can be accessed by the students outside of the classroom.

Teaching Note: Core activity download and get endstar working please share the log in details for endstar with your class.

Teaching Note: Have students complete the activities and then upload their progress to classkick.

Teaching note: If a student completes everything in the ‘All Students’ row, they have achieved the lesson objective. The ‘Many’ and ‘Some’ levels are upward extensions, not mandatory requirements.

slides.content:
Mandatory Diagnostic

Please scan the QR code or click the link below to begin your diagnostic test. This assessment helps us understand your current knowledge and is required for all students.

Important: This test is not part of your grade, but it is mandatory. 
Please complete it under strict exam conditions: no use of AI tools or web search is allowed. Your results will be shared with you by your teacher.

Click here to start the assessment

The Explorers Programme

1

Introduction to Game Development

Level 1

Navigating the Endstar Platform
Welcome to your first step as a game creator. 
Today, you will learn how to access Endstar, explore the core tools, and begin working like a real game designer.

Lesson 1

Lesson 1 Starter 
AI Sounds Smart — But Does It Understand?

What do I do?

Ask the AI: "Explain how to build a simple game environment in Endstar."

Think

Does the answer sound confident?

Does sounding confident mean understanding?

Try

Open Endstar.

Test one instruction from the AI.

Think. Pair. Share

Think

Prepare Response Collect key points and evidence

Pair

Practice Delivery. Refine wording and timing

Share Confidently

Present clearly and listen

AI can explain things clearly without understanding them.

Humans must test and decide.

AI Literacy Focus: Understanding AI limits (OECD K1.3)

The Explorers Programme

1

Download and open Endstar. Navigate the basics of Endstar.

Lesson Objectives

Use AI responsibly to ask questions about game development and Endstar.

Who are Endless Studios?

Endless Studios is a youth-focused game-making and learning studio. Founded as a partnership between Endless and E-Line Media.

Vision: Building games provides individuals with tools for personal growth and contributes to the economic development of entire regions.

Endstar is an innovative game-making platform designed to enhance students' creative and critical thinking skills, fully immersing them in the game development process.

What is Endstar?

Overall Long-Term Explorer Programme: Aims for Students

Aim 1: Creative Mastery Through Game Development
Equip students with the ability to design, build, and iterate on original digital products using Endstar, SchoolAI, and emerging AI tools—developing real technical fluency in game design, logic, systems thinking, and digital creation.

Aim 2: AI Literacy and Responsible Digital Innovation
Ensure every student develops a foundational understanding of how AI works, how to use it responsibly, and how to integrate AI into the creative process—building the future-ready mindset required for an AI-enabled world.

Aim 3: Problem-Solving, Systems Thinking, and Iteration
Develop students’ capacity to analyse problems, test solutions, debug challenges, and refine ideas through structured iteration. Students learn to approach complexity logically and creatively—mirroring real-world design and engineering workflows.

Aim 4: Collaboration, Communication & Professional Skills
Strengthen collaboration and clear communication through teamwork, pitching, peer feedback, and cross-functional problem solving—reflecting the roles and expectations of modern creative-tech industries.

Aim 5: Student Agency, Confidence & Portfolio Development
Empower students to build real digital portfolios showcasing their skills, creativity, and growth. Students gain confidence by presenting their ideas, defending decisions, and reflecting on their learning journey.

Aim 6: Pathways to Future Careers & Lifelong Learning
Expose students to UAE-relevant industries—game design, AI, digital media, creative technologies—cultivating curiosity, ambition, and a commitment to continued learning. Students leave the programme more adaptable, motivated, and workforce-ready.

Step 1

Go to https://about.endlessstudios.com/
Click ‘The Studio’ on the top right
Click ‘Log in’ on the top right of the dashboard
Enter your allocated username and password

In the following slide a video will detail everything you need to know.

Activity

Activity:  Open and Navigate Endstar

Students must be able to open Endstar, navigate all functions of Endstar and be prepared to answer questions about the platform as part of this introductory activity. 
This activity is a basic ‘get to know’ the Endstar programme.

Portfolio Evidence — Lesson 1
What you built + how you used AI

Criteria: We know we are successful when….

All students must: 
✔ Log in successfully to Endstar
✔ Navigate the basic interface (toolbar, camera, workspace)
✔ Upload one screenshot of their environment to their Portfolio

Many students will: 
✔ Identify the purpose of key tools (move, tile, prop, erase, inspect)
✔ Adjust essential settings for smoother performance (resolution, fullscreen, mouse sensitivity)
✔ Use AI to ask at least one productive question about the platform (e.g., “What does this tool do?”)

Some students may: 
✔ Explain how Endstar’s UI compares to other platforms they’ve used
✔ Discover advanced properties (scale, rotation, object attributes)
✔ Use AI thoughtfully—evaluating whether its suggestions are accurate, useful, or biased

Assessment For Learning

Plenary
Game Development
GD-01.1
Which action is most likely to reduce performance issues in an Endstar environment?
A. Adding more props to make the level detailed
B. Removing unused objects and testing regularly
C. Using only default tiles
D. Avoiding saving until the end
GD-01.2
Which tool is most appropriate for removing an object that is no longer needed?
A. Move Tool
B. Tile Tool
C. Prop Tool
D. Erase Tool

Assessment For Learning

Plenary
AI Literacy (OECD K1.3)
AI-01.1
Why can AI give confident instructions that do not fully work in Endstar?
A. AI understands tools but chooses shortcuts
B. AI checks Endstar before answering
C. AI predicts language patterns, not real tool behaviour
D. AI follows outdated rules
AI-01.2
What is the best role for a student when using AI in Lesson 1?
A. Follow AI steps exactly
B. Ask AI to repeat answers
C. Test AI suggestions and decide what works
D. Avoid AI completely

OECD Mapping: Knowledge K1.3 (Understanding AI limits and reliability)

slides.content:
Introduction to Game Development Environment

Level 1

Essential Tools & Beginner Environment Design
CORE CONCEPT
Students learn how to select a tile, shape an environment, and begin building a simple playable layout in Endstar.

Lesson 2

Lesson 2 Starter 
If AI Sounds Confident, Should We Trust It?

What do I do?

Ask the AI: "What is the most important tool in Endstar and why?"

Think

Is there only one correct answer?
Would different designers choose different tools?

Try

Look at Endstar.
Decide if you agree with the AI — or not.

Think. Pair. Share

Think

Prepare Response Collect key points and evidence

Pair

Practice Delivery. Refine wording and timing

Share Confidently

Present clearly and listen

Confidence does not always mean correctness.
Human judgement still matters.

AI Literacy Focus: Fluency ≠ understanding (OECD K1.3)

The Explorers Programme

1

Lesson Starter

What Do I Do?

Log into Endstar and open your Level 1 project

Recap the tools from Lesson 1 (Move, Tile, Prop, Erase, Inspect)

What is one improvement you want to make to your environment today?

First

Next

Then

Use AI to generate or refine an environment idea.

Select a tile and use it to design a simple environment. 
Use Endstar tools more confidently (tile, prop, move, erase). 
Build and test a playable environment layout.

Lesson Objectives

Key Features of a Strong Level Layout

A good environment often includes:
	•	Clear paths for the player
	•	Various heights and shapes
	•	Safe starting areas
	•	Interesting obstacles
	•	A goal or destination

What Makes a Good Beginner Environment?

Do you Remember the In-Game Tools from Last Lesson?

Students should become familiar with the following tools in the gamer interface:

Empty Tool (for navigation)
Terrain Tool (for placing terrain)
Prop Tool (for placing new objects)
Erase Tool (for removing objects)
Wiring Tool (for connections)
Inspector Tool (for changing properties)
Copy Tool (for duplicating objects)
Move Tool (for repositioning)

Retrieval Activity

Studio Video Link

In-Game Tools Recap

This video helps to:

Recap over the different in-game tools available
Revise the ability each tool has
Develop an understanding of where each tool can be used

Video Guidance

Question 1:  Which tool did you use most in Lesson 1 and why?

Question 2:  	How does tile choice impact how a player moves through the environment?

Question 3:  What is one way you could make your environment more interesting?

Let’s Discuss

Activity

Build a larger environment using:
✔ Tiles
✔ Props
✔ Move / Erase tools

Assessment For Learning

Plenary
Game Development
GD-02.1
A player feels lost while playing a level. Which design choice best improves navigation?
A. Increasing enemy difficulty
B. Adding random props
C. Using clear pathways and landmarks
D. Making the level larger
GD-02.2
Why is consistency important in environment design?
A. It makes the game shorter
B. It helps players understand where to go and what to do
C. It reduces file size
D. It limits creativity

Assessment For Learning

Plenary
AI Literacy (OECD K1.3)
AI-02.1
AI suggests an environment layout that looks good but plays poorly. What does this show?
A. AI prefers artistic designs
B. AI cannot experience gameplay like a human player
C. The engine is faulty
D. The player misunderstood the rules
AI-02.2
Which action best shows healthy scepticism when using AI for environment ideas?
A. Using the first idea given
B. Asking for more detail
C. Testing ideas inside Endstar before keeping them
D. Copying ideas from popular games

metadata.activity_description:
Endstar lets students creatively connect objects to build mechanisms. You could design a simple lever to open a door or create more complex setups like moving an object onto a pressure plate to achieve the same result.

It's your task to come up with innovative mechanisms to accomplish the goal of your game.

Activity 1:  Create a Complex Game Pathway in Endstar

Students should create a linear pathway using connected elements. The environment should be navigable solely by moving through the links they've established.

This could involve a range of door mechanisms and/or intricate puzzles.

✔ Design a basic navigable pathway

✔ Use one type of door mechanism to control access

✔ Ensure the pathway works from start to finish

✔ Upload a screenshot or short demo to portfolio

✔ Integrate more than one door mechanism

✔ Add a simple puzzle or challenge linked to the pathway

✔ Show how doors and pathway work together

✔ Create a more complex environment with multiple routes

✔ Use a variety of door mechanisms and layered puzzles

✔ Test and refine the pathway based on playtesting or feedback

Teaching Note: Have students complete the activities and then upload their progress to classkick.
assessment_signals:
GD-03.1
What is a major risk of designing a pathway with only one possible route?

GD-03.2
Why might branching paths improve player experience?

AI-03.1
Why should students test AI-suggested pathways?

AI-03.2
What limitation of AI is revealed when a pathway works logically but feels unfair?

slides.content:
Core Game Mechanics & Tools

Level 2

Understanding Core Game Mechanics

Lesson 3

Lesson 3 Starter

Why Does AI Give Different Answers?

What do I do?

Ask the AI: "Give me three ideas for a simple platformer game."

Compare

Share answers with a partner.
Are the ideas the same or different?

Think

Did the AI "think again"?
Or did it predict something else?

AI predicts what is likely, not what is correct.Different answers are normal.

AI Predicts Likelihood

Probabilistic Outputs

Models estimate probabilities

Context Matters

Input changes outcomes

Not Always Correct

Predictions can be wrong

Multiple Valid Answers

Lesson 5 Starter

Does Data Always Tell the Truth?

What do I do?

Look at two different platformer games.

Decide which one you think would be more popular — and why.

Think

What evidence are you using to decide?

Are you guessing, or using real information?

Compare

Now ask the AI: “Which of these games would be more popular, and why?”

Compare the AI’s reasoning with your own.

Data does not explain itself.

Humans decide what evidence matters.

AI Literacy Focus: Bias, data limits & audience context (OECD K1.4)

The Explorer's Programme

How to complete market research on selected games

Learn the basics of Market Research as an Entrepreneur

Lesson Objectives

What is Market Research?

As a software developer, game creator, or entrepreneur, it's essential to understand the market you aim to serve. Market research involves analyzing the target market for your game to comprehend:


The needs and preferences of target customers: Who is the person you will sell to and what do they like/dislike?

Assess market trends: What is popular with people and how do your consumers behave?

Evaluate the competition: What is already out there and what is your USP (Unique Selling Point)?

Why is it Crucial to Accurately Define the Target Audience?

In a game-making studio, it's crucial to clearly define your target audience. Knowing your audience means analyzing key factors to ensure that your game or product connects with the right people and meets their needs.


Who is your audience - Think about who you want to reach. What are their habits, likes, and values? What kind of people are they?

How can you adapt your product - How can you change or improve your game or product to fit what your audience wants and needs?

Why is knowing your audience important - When you understand your audience, you can create something they enjoy more, which leads to better engagement, more satisfaction, and greater success.

Why Market Research is Essential…

Step 1

Correct Audience Targeting: Ensures your game reaches the right players.
Avoiding Past Mistakes: Helps you learn from the failures and shortcomings in similar products.
Valuable Feedback: Provides insights into what players find engaging and successful.
Efficiency in Resources: Prevents wasting time and resources by guiding your development with informed feedback.

How do I do Market Research?

Identify the Target Audience: Who is this game targeted for?


Gather Information About Popular Games: What makes this a popular game to play?


Analyze Features That Make These Games Successful: What features do you think would also be successful in your game?


Collect Feedback: What do your classmates think of the game?


Compile and Analyze Data: Look for mentioned features that you should consider further.

Diverse Skill Sets: Just like in professional game studios, different talents (like programming, design, and storytelling) come together to create a richer and more complex game.

Enhanced Creativity: Teams often generate more creative ideas than individuals alone, as collaboration fosters innovation and problem-solving.

Efficiency: In the industry, tasks are divided among team members to speed up development and tackle complex projects effectively.

Peer Review: Regular feedback within a team helps improve game quality, as different perspectives can spot issues and opportunities that one person might miss.

Resilience: Working in teams provides support and motivation, which is crucial during challenging phases of game development.

The Explorer's Programme

What is Product-Market Fit and Why is it Needed?

How can you ensure your game achieves Product-Market Fit?

What is needed to make an effective team?

Lesson Objectives

Step 1

What is Product-Market Fit and Why is it Needed?

Product-Market Fit is the process of ensuring that your product meets the needs and desires of your target market. 

Achieving product-market fit is crucial for the success of a product because it means that the product is something that customers want and will buy.

Find Your Customers: Determine who your potential customers are and understand their needs and preferences.

Check the Problem: Make sure the problem your product solves is significant for your target market and that your solution is appealing to them.

Test Your Product: Create a simple version of your product and get feedback from a few customers to see if it works well and meets their needs.

Improve Your Product: Use the feedback to make your product better and fix any problems to better meet the needs of your target market.

See How It Sells: Release your product to a small group of people to see how they like it, and make changes if needed before a big launch.

What is Product-Market Fit (PMF)?

Here’s what you need to do to achieve Product-Market Fit:
Product Market Fit Explained

Desert Quest

In the UAE, a passionate team of young game developers created "Desert Quest," an epic adventure game set in the mystical Arabian Desert. The game was designed specifically for teenagers who love exploring hidden secrets and solving challenging puzzles. Before even starting development, the developers spent time talking to students in schools, asking them about their favorite game elements and what would excite them most in a new game.

Quiz Time!

Do you think they achieved product market fit?

Understanding Players: The developers included elements that UAE students loved.
Testing the Game: They released an early version of the game to a small group of students and eagerly gathered feedback.
Improving Based on Feedback: The team eagerly implemented the feedback, adding new levels, characters, and more exciting challenges.
Effective Launch: When they launched the final game, it was a massive success. Students felt the game was uniquely tailored for them.

Yes, they did! But why did they succeed?

Space  Adventure

Another driven group of developers embarked on creating "Space Adventure," an ambitious sci-fi game set in the vast expanse of outer space. They were confident it would be a blockbuster, riding the wave of global popularity for space games. They took a different approach and decided not to engage with UAE students to understand their preferences but went ahead with what believed would work.

Ready for another one?

Do you think they achieved product market fit?

Not Understanding Players: The developers assumed UAE students would automatically love the game without asking them.
Skipping Feedback: They launched the game without testing it with local students, missing out on crucial feedback.
Poor Engagement: The game lacked elements that resonated with UAE students,  such as familiar cultural stories or relatable settings. It felt disconnected and failed to capture their interest.
Unsuccessful Launch: When "Space Adventure" launched, it struggled to gain attention. Players quickly lost interest, and the game failed to achieve popularity.

No, they didn’t! But why didn’t they succeed?

YOU cannot do this alone

Step 2

Game studio members don't work alone; diverse skill sets are essential.

Step 2 of today's lesson involves finding those crucial team members to ensure your success. This is a mandatory step for progressing successfully in this program.

Make your Studio - Form a Team of 2-3

Review Your Results: Look back at what you've discovered during your market research.

Identify Similarities: Find classmates whose research outcomes align with yours.

Discuss Goals: Talk about what each of you hopes to achieve in your game development.

Form Teams: Partner up with those who share similar interests and goals to enhance collaboration.

A game studio doesn’t work alone, and you won’t either. Review the steps below and let's form your team.

The Story Teller: Who will ensure that your story is engaging?

The Expert Creator: Who will expertly craft this engaging story?

The Artist: Emotional art is key to storytelling. Who is your artist?

(Optional) The Project Manager: With all these moving parts, who will ensure you meet your deadlines?

Who’s in your team?

Your game studio should have a diverse skill set. It's important to include team members who are skilled in the following areas:

Why Work in Teams?

Diverse Skill Sets: Just like in professional game studios, different talents (like programming, design, and storytelling) come together to create a richer and more complex game.

Enhanced Creativity: Teams often generate more creative ideas than individuals alone, as collaboration fosters innovation and problem-solving.

Efficiency: In the industry, tasks are divided among team members to speed up development and tackle complex projects effectively.

Peer Review: Regular feedback within a team helps improve game quality, as different perspectives can spot issues and opportunities that one person might miss.

Resilience: Working in teams provides support and motivation, which is crucial during challenging phases of game development.
Game Studio Names

Question 1:  Why can’t a game studio consist of JUST one person?

Question 2:  What different skills are within your team?

Question 3:  What is the most important aspect for your team to focus on?

Let’s Discuss

Step 3

How can you ensure your game achieves Product-Market Fit?

Ensuring that you achieve product-market fit can be very challenging and requires a lot of patience.

However, it is crucial for your game's success because it ensures that your game is something players will enjoy and choose to play.

Activity 1:  Market Matchmaker

Using the headings provided in your workbook, students will revisit the game they reviewed last lesson. They will apply these headings to determine whether the game matches the market's needs. Based on this evaluation, students can then draw conclusions on how they might adjust the game to better achieve market fit.

metadata.activity_description:
First, you identify who would want your pearls. Are they tourists looking for unique souvenirs, local jewelers wanting to create exclusive jewelry, or high-end collectors searching for rare pieces?

Do you think tourists or local people would be more interested in buying your pearls? Explain why you think one group might prefer them more.

How would you sell your pearls differently to a local person compared to a tourist? Think about what each group might like or need.

Next, you determine what makes your pearls special. Perhaps they have a unique color or shine that no other pearls have. You craft a story about why these pearls are valuable and how they can add beauty and elegance to any jewelry piece.

Can you create a short story that makes your pearls seem special and unique? What details would you include to show how different your pearls are?

What specific parts of your story would you emphasize to make your pearls stand out? Why are these parts important to your story?

Then, you decide how to get your pearls to the customers. Will you sell them at a bustling market in Dubai, through an online store, or by partnering with luxury jewelry stores?

Based on what you've learned about choosing the right customers, where do you think is the best place to sell your amazing pearls?

Why is this distribution channel the best choice for your pearls?

Finally, you plan how to sell your pearls. Will you showcase them at local markets with a beautiful display, run advertisements online, or arrange exclusive previews for high-end clients?

Where do you think is the best place to sell your pearls, and why do you think this place would be perfect?

What parts of your pearl’s story would you highlight to make them appealing to buy? Why are these important?

Step 2 is designed to help you transition from developing your game to planning its launch. This task is straightforward but critical, as it requires you to align your Go-To-Market strategy with the theme and goals of your game.

Remember, the theme of your game should shine through in your marketing efforts.

Activity 1:  Making your team game’s GTM

Together, as a team, you need to start planning your game's launch. Your Go-To-Market strategy should be crafted in a way that aligns with your game’s theme and objectives.

Think about who your ideal players are, what message will grab their attention, and how you can make the introduction of your game an exciting event that they won’t want to miss.

✔ State whether the game meets the basic needs of its audience

✔ Give at least one reason for their decision

✔ Record feedback clearly in the workbook

✔ Give detailed feedback on the game’s key features

✔ Explain how features appeal to the target audience

✔ Suggest simple improvements

✔ Provide comprehensive feedback across multiple areas

✔ Evaluate audience fit, unique selling points, and player appeal

✔ Suggest specific strategies to improve market success

Lesson Objectives

Step 1

Understanding Go-To-Market (GTM)

Today's lesson is focused on understanding the Go-To-Market (GTM) strategy. This step is essential for launching your product successfully in the market.

Remember, a strong GTM strategy aligns your product with market needs and creates a clear path to reach your customers.

Target Market: Identify who your customers are and understand their needs.

Value Proposition: Explain why your product is valuable and how it solves the customers' problems.

Distribution Channels: Determine how you will deliver your product to your customers (e.g., online, in-store).

Pricing Strategy: Decide how much you will charge for your product to attract customers and ensure profitability.

Sales Strategy: Plan how you will sell your product, including the sales tactics and processes you will use.

What is involved in Go-To-Market?

As an entrepreneur, understanding a Go-To-Market strategy is crucial for launching your product successfully. Here’s a breakdown of what GTM involves:

Example: Selling Pearls

Imagine you are a pearl merchant in the sunny UAE. One day, while diving, you find something incredible—a seabed full of glowing pearls. These pearls shine brightly, like stars in the night sky. In your eyes, these are not ordinary pearls; they glow with a special light that no one has ever seen before.

Excited about your discovery, you decide to create a plan to sell these glowing pearls to people all around the world. This plan is called a Go-To-Market (GTM) strategy.


What do you think you need to be successful in your GTM strategy?

Target Market:


First, you identify who would want your pearls. Are they tourists looking for unique souvenirs, local jewelers wanting to create exclusive jewelry, or high-end collectors searching for rare pieces?


Let’s Discuss: 

Do you think tourists or local people would be more interested in buying your pearls? Explain why you think one group might prefer them more.

How would you sell your pearls differently to a local person compared to a tourist? Think about what each group might like or need.

Value Proposition:


Next, you determine what makes your pearls special. Perhaps they have a unique color or shine that no other pearls have. You craft a story about why these pearls are valuable and how they can add beauty and elegance to any jewelry piece.


Let’s Discuss: 

Can you create a short story that makes your pearls seem special and unique? What details would you include to show how different your pearls are?

What specific parts of your story would you emphasize to make your pearls stand out? Why are these parts important to your story?

Distribution Channels:


Then, you decide how to get your pearls to the customers. Will you sell them at a bustling market in Dubai, through an online store, or by partnering with luxury jewelry stores?


Let’s Discuss: 

Based on what you've learned about choosing the right customers, where do you think is the best place to sell your amazing pearls?

Why is this distribution channel the best choice for your pearls?

Sales Strategy:


Finally, you plan how to sell your pearls. Will you showcase them at local markets with a beautiful display, run advertisements online, or arrange exclusive previews for high-end clients?


Let’s Discuss: 

Where do you think is the best place to sell your pearls, and why do you think this place would be perfect?

What parts of your pearl’s story would you highlight to make them appealing to buy? Why are these important?

Go-Market-Strategy

metadata.activity_description:
Activity 1:  Build a Channel Strategy

Students will choose one or more marketing channels (e.g., Instagram, TikTok, etc.) to promote their game. They will then design a sample post or advertisement for one of the selected channels.

In addition, students should be able to explain why they chose that particular channel and why this strategy would be the most effective for their game launch.

✔ Choose at least one marketing platform (e.g. Instagram, TikTok)

✔ Create one simple post or ad for the chosen platform

✔ Explain why this platform fits the game and target audience

✔ Upload post/ad design to portfolio

✔ Choose multiple platforms

✔ Create two posts or ads per platform

✔ Explain how each platform helps reach the target audience

✔ Show how posts improve game visibility

✔ Design an advanced channel strategy across platforms

✔ Use ideas such as influencers, targeted ads, or cross-posting

✔ Create highly creative and immersive promotional content

Activity 1:  Develop a Marketing Plan

As you’ve seen from the sample, a successful marketing plan requires a variety of key elements. Now, students must create a marketing plan for a different game, which could even be one of their own creations.

Use the sample as a guide, but feel free to be creative and think outside the box to make your game stand out!

✔ Identify the game’s target audience

✔ Explain the core value of the game

✔ Choose at least one marketing channel

✔ Create a basic promotional idea

✔ Use multiple marketing channels

✔ Design clear promotional strategies for each channel

✔ Explain how these strategies increase visibility

✔ Create an advanced launch plan

✔ Include strategies such as influencers, events, or partnerships

✔ Design a coordinated campaign that maximises engagement

Teaching Note: Have students complete the activities and then upload their progress to classkick.

Lesson 8 Starter

Just Because You Can, Should You?

What do I do?

Ask the AI: "What is the best platform to market a game to teenagers?"

Think

Who might be excluded by this choice?
Are reach and impact the same thing?

Responsible design considers people, not just numbers.

AI Literacy Focus: Ethics, responsibility & audience awareness (OECD K1.4)

The Explorer's Programme

Decide

Choose a marketing channel — and explain why it is appropriate, not just effective.

Understand the importance of marketing channels for a game launch.

Develop a marketing plan that integrates multiple promotional methods.

Lesson Objectives

Step 1

Understanding Marketing Channels

Definition: Marketing channels are platforms where you promote and distribute your game to reach your audience.

Types of Channels:

Social Media (e.g., Instagram, Twitter)
Influencers and Streamers
Game Review Websites
Paid Advertising (e.g., Google Ads, Facebook Ads)
Email Campaigns
Community Engagement (e.g., Reddit, Discord)

Example 1: “Indie Game A” used a successful influencer campaign to build pre-launch hype.


Example 2: “AAA Game B” relied on paid ads and community forums to engage core players.



Discussion Point: How do different channels target different segments of your audience?

Examples of Game Launch Marketing

Game Launch Marketing are strategies and activities used to promote a game leading up to and during its release. This can include social media campaigns, trailers, influencer partnerships, and pre-release events, all aimed at generating buzz and attracting players.

Activity 1:  Build a Channel Strategy

Students will choose one or more marketing channels (e.g., Instagram, TikTok, etc.) to promote their game. They will then design a sample post or advertisement for one of the selected channels.

In addition, students should be able to explain why they chose that particular channel and why this strategy would be the most effective for their game launch.

Activity

All students must: 
✔ Choose at least one marketing platform (e.g. Instagram, TikTok)
✔ Create one simple post or ad for the chosen platform
✔ Explain why this platform fits the game and target audience
✔ Upload post/ad design to portfolio
Many students will: 
✔ Choose multiple platforms
✔ Create two posts or ads per platform
✔ Explain how each platform helps reach the target audience
✔ Show how posts improve game visibility

Some students may: 
✔ Design an advanced channel strategy across platforms
✔ Use ideas such as influencers, targeted ads, or cross-posting
✔ Create highly creative and immersive promotional content

Criteria

Step 2

What is a Marketing Plan?

A marketing plan is like your game’s roadmap to success! It’s a strategy that helps you figure out how to promote and sell your product to the right audience. Think of it as a way to tell the world why they should play your game and how to get it in their hands. 

In the UAE, for example, big events like GITEX showcase new tech and games, with companies creating marketing plans to stand out and attract players. 

Your marketing plan will do the same, making sure your game gets noticed and played!

Example 1: "Mobile App X” created a content strategy using Instagram reels and influencer collaborations to drive downloads.


Example 2: "Tech Product X” built a marketing plan around Facebook ads, email campaigns, and a launch event to maximize visibility.



Discussion Point: How can different elements of a marketing plan help reach your audience more effectively?

Examples of a Marketing Plan

A marketing plan is a detailed strategy for promoting a product or service. It outlines how to reach your target audience, the key messages you want to convey, and the channels you'll use, such as social media, ads, and partnerships.

Grab the marketing plan sample 

HERE

Sample Marketing Plan

Sample App/Game: How could we effectively market a niche game such as ‘Pokemon Go’?

Activity 1:  Develop a Marketing Plan

As you’ve seen from the sample, a successful marketing plan requires a variety of key elements. Now, students must create a marketing plan for a different game, which could even be one of their own creations.

Use the sample as a guide, but feel free to be creative and think outside the box to make your game stand out!

Activity

Portfolio Evidence — Lesson 8
What you built + how you used AI

All students must:
✔ Identify the game’s target audience
✔ Explain the core value of the game
✔ Choose at least one marketing channel
✔ Create a basic promotional idea

Many students will: 
✔ Use multiple marketing channels
✔ Design clear promotional strategies for each channel
✔ Explain how these strategies increase visibility

Some students may: 
✔ Create an advanced launch plan
✔ Include strategies such as influencers, events, or partnerships
✔ Design a coordinated campaign that maximises engagement

B. Academic forums

D. Internal testing tools

D. Whether competitors use it fairly

What risk exists when relying only on AI to select marketing channels?

B. The AI may suggest unethical or inappropriate placements

What Makes a Story Feel Like a Game?

What do I do?

Think

What challenge does the player face?

Discuss

How is this different from a story in a book or film?

Game stories are experienced through actions, not just words.

Game Design Focus: Narrative ideation & storyboarding (OECD K1.3)

The Explorer's Programme

1

How to brainstorm a game idea

Creating a comic-style game storyline

Lesson Objectives

Think of a platformer game like a road trip. 

Without a story, it’s like driving down a straight road—fun at first, but eventually, it becomes dull with no challenges or engagement. 

The real excitement comes from the twists, turns, and surprises along the journey.

Create your Story

The Beginning: How will you immediately hook the player? Think about games like Metal Gear Solid—how does the story instantly provide depth and intrigue?

The Middle: What kind of world is your character exploring? Describe the setting that keeps the player engaged.

Challenges/Villains: Who or what is standing in your character’s way? Why are they against your character?

The Ending: How does your story conclude? Like Super Mario rescuing the princess, what’s the ultimate goal or resolution for your character?

How do I create a theme/story?

Creating a story isn't something we do alone—it’s time to work as a team! Think of your story like an essay you're writing with friends. Everyone has a role to play:

Activity

Creating an effective storyline for your game is essential. In this activity, you’ll collaborate as a team to plan and creatively communicate your storyline. You can do this using Mural or physical sticky notes, whichever works best for your group.

Example

Step 2

To make a successful game, you need to be an inspiring storyteller. Keep in mind the four tips below:

Define your theme
Develop your characters
Plan player choices
Make the adventure challenging and engaging

Become a Story-Teller

What is Game Ideation?

As a game developer or designer, ideation is a pivotal stage in the game-making process. It involves creative brainstorming and conceptualization to form the core elements of your game. 

During this stage, it's crucial to focus on several key aspects:

Identifying the game's theme and setting
Designing core gameplay mechanics
Creating engaging narratives
Defining art style and aesthetics
Mapping user experience

Ideation Aspect Meanings:

Identifying the game's theme and setting: What is the overall concept of your game? Decide on a unique setting and theme that will engage your target audience.
Designing core gameplay mechanics: What are the crucial interactions within the game? Define the basic mechanics, such as combat, puzzle-solving, or strategy, that will drive the player experience.
Creating engaging narratives: Will your game have a storyline? If so, sketch out the basic plot and character stories. Consider how the narrative will unfold as players progress through the game.
Defining art style and aesthetics: What visual style will best represent your game? Explore different art directions and establish a consistent aesthetic that aligns with your theme.
Mapping user experience: How will players interact with the game? Think about the user interface, controls, and overall user experience to ensure it’s intuitive and enjoyable.

Activity

Activity 2:  Create Your Game as a Comic

Use the student workbook to draw key scenes and challenges from your game in a comic strip format. Add dialogues and short descriptions to each scene to bring your story to life. This activity will help you clearly outline your game’s progression and share your creative vision.

“In two sentences, explain what a core gameplay loop is in a platformer and why it matters.”

“For our [theme] platformer, design a simple three-step loop with action, reward, and progression.”

“Suggest one twist to add risk–reward tension to that loop.”

Students will prompt Ana to explain gameplay loops, design a three-step loop for their chosen theme, and refine it with a tension-building twist, then sketch and share their final loops.

Expansion - How does the game change?

6-11 Framework - What emotion and instincts are you basing it on?

Level 5

Lesson 10

Lesson 10 Starter

Why Do Players Keep Playing?

1

What do I do?

Play a short game segment or imagine a familiar game. Focus on what makes it engaging.

2

Think

What action does the player perform repeatedly? What immediate reward or feedback follows that action?

3

Sketch

Draw a simple loop illustrating the relationship: Action → Reward → Progress. How does each step lead to the next?

Good games repeat actions — but change the experience.

Mastery comes from continuous engagement.

Game Design Focus: Gameplay loops & player motivation

The Explorer's Programme

“Game reward systems are controlled by algorithms that decide what players get. If those rules aren’t clear or fair, players can lose trust.”

Ask AI - Algorithmic Transparency

Fact

Relevance

Ask AI

Students designing gameplay loops should think about how to communicate those rules, so players feel rewards are earned and the experience is fair.

How can you make sure players understand your risk–reward loop?
What could happen if a reward system feels unfair or hidden?

Create a gameplay loop for your game idea

How to create a gameplay loop with emotion and instinct

Lesson Objectives

What is Gameplay Loop?

Step 1

Action: are the “what you are doing” part of gameplay.


Reward:  are the things players get for doing actions. 


Expansion: is how the game changes based on player actions and the rewards they earn.

There are three important aspects to remember when creating a gameplay loop:

Gameplay Loop Example

Reward

Action

Expansion

A Lens into Gameplay Through Emotions

Your feelings - like fear, excitement, or anticipation, drive your Actions in the game. 

The rules of the game allow this action, like being able to dodge an attack when you run.

Taking that action changes what happens next in the game - maybe the enemy attacks and misses you. This is what we call Gameplay. 

The Gameplay with an Objective creates an Experience, like feeling relieved but still a bit panicked, which influences your next action.”

Game Narrative and Level Design

Level 6

Crafting Engaging Game Narratives

Lesson 11

Lesson 11 Starter

Whose Story Is It Anyway?

What do I do?

Write one sentence describing your game’s world or main character.

Think

Who is this story for?
What do you want the player to feel?

Compare (Optional)

Ask AI: “Suggest a story idea for this type of game.”

Compare it with your own idea.

AI can suggest stories.

Humans decide meaning and emotion.

AI Literacy Focus: Human creativity & authorship (OECD K1.3)

The Explorer's Programme

“Explainable AI means creating artificial intelligence that people can easily understand and trust, even when it makes complex decisions.”

Ask AI - Explainable AI

Fact

Relevance

Ask AI

Students writing a game story hook need to explain their idea clearly, just like AI systems must explain their choices to people in a simple way.

How could a good story hook help someone quickly “get” your game?
What lessons can you learn from explainable AI when presenting creative ideas?

Create a gameplay loop for YOUR game idea?

What is a Game Narrative?

Lesson Objectives

What is a Game Narrative?

A game narrative refers to the art and technique of crafting stories within video games. 

A game narrative refers to the story or plot within the game.
It includes characters, the setting, and the sequence of events that unfold.
The narrative gives the player a reason to continue playing, providing purpose and motivation to achieve in-game goals.

Question:

How might the absence of a narrative affect a player's overall experience in a game, and why do you think that is?

Why is Game Narrative Important?

Engagement: The narrative is crucial for keeping players engaged and invested in the game.
Emotional Connection: Create emotional connections with characters, making the player care about what happens next.
Make Choices: The story also drives the gameplay forward, influencing the choices and actions a player takes.

Question:

Can you think of a game where the story made you care about a character or event?

Without an engaging game narrative, the player will find it difficult to fully immerse themselves into the game.

Elements of an Engaging Narrative

Characters: Well-developed heroes, villains, or non-playable characters (NPCs) that interact with the player.
Setting: The environment or world where the game takes place, such as a fantasy land, a post-apocalyptic city, or outer space.
Conflict: The central challenge or problem the characters must overcome.
Progression: How the story evolves as the player advances through the game, with increasing stakes and complexity.

Question:

How do the elements of characters, setting, conflict, and progression work together to create a compelling game narrative?

Key Ingredients of a Good Character

Give them goals: Each character should have clear goals or motivations that drive their actions within the game.
Add flaws: Flawed characters feel more human and relatable, making them more engaging for players.
Create backstory: Every character should have a history that influences their current decisions and behavior in the game.

Question:

How do a character’s goals and flaws make them more relatable to players?

Creating a Game World

Location: Decide whether the game is set in a futuristic city, a medieval kingdom, a space station, or any other setting.
Rules: Define the logic or rules of the game world. Is magic possible? Are there advanced technologies? Do natural laws apply?
History: Consider what happened before the game starts, including historical events that may affect the world or characters now.

Question:

How would changing the rules or setting of a game world impact the overall narrative and gameplay?

Crafting Conflict

Internal Conflict: Challenges within the character, such as personal struggles or difficult decisions.
External Conflict: Conflicts between the character and outside forces, such as villains, nature, or society.
Multiple conflicts: Combining both internal and external conflicts creates a deeper, more engaging narrative for the player.

Question:

How does the balance between internal and external conflicts shape a character’s development throughout the game?

Narrative Progression

Acts: Divide the story into three main parts—beginning, middle, and end, each with a clear purpose and development.
Choices: Allow players to make decisions that affect the outcome of the game, giving them agency in the story.
Consequences: Every choice should have meaningful consequences, shaping the narrative based on what the player decides.

Question:

How can giving players choices in a game’s narrative impact their experience?

Creating a Compelling Game Narrative

Concept

Characters

Start with a clear concept that defines the core emotion or theme.

World

Pace

Create multi-dimensional and relatable characters with clear motivations, strengths, and flaws.

Craft a rich and detailed game world with its own history, culture, and politics.

Maintain a good narrative pace to keep players engaged.

Lesson 12

Lesson 12 Starter

Can You See the Level Before You Build It?

What do I do?

Sketch a top-down or side-view map of a game level on paper.

Think

Where does the player start?
Where are the challenges and safe spaces?

Explain

Add notes showing why each area exists and what purpose it serves in the player's journey.

Good levels are planned before they are built.

Game Design Focus: Level layout & spatial reasoning (OECD K1.1)

The Explorer's Programme

“Responsible AI means designing technology that is fair, transparent, and respects the needs of all its users.”

Ask AI - Responsible AI Design

Fact

Relevance

Ask AI

When designing a level, you should think about how players experience routes, obstacles, and checkpoints — just as a responsible AI should consider user fairness and accessibility.

How could you design your level so players of all skill levels can enjoy it?
What steps could you take to avoid making your level too hard or confusing for new players?

Create a Level Layout for your Game

The Basics of Level Design

Lesson Objectives

What is Level Design?

Step 1

Level design is a critical element of game development, involving three key elements:

Layout and Structure
Gameplay Mechanics
Pacing and Flow

Your goal is create an engaging and interactive environment for player to enjoy.

Level Design - Key Elements

Layout and Structure: Designing the physical space where the game takes place, including the arrangement of obstacles, pathways, and interactive elements

Gameplay Mechanics: Integrating game rules and mechanics into the level to ensure a smooth and enjoyable player experience.

Pacing and Flow: Balancing the difficulty and progression of the level to keep players engaged without overwhelming them.

“Add a hidden shortcut between the second and third combat arenas and move the first checkpoint to just after the first puzzle.”
“Suggest a memorable landmark at the level’s midpoint to help players orient themselves.

Students will prompt AI to generate a full level blueprint, identify start/end points, main and optional routes, checkpoints, and obstacles, then refine their design with targeted AI tweaks.

AI as an Assistant

Why is Level Design Essential?

Gameplay

Storytelling

Engagement

Challenge

Immersion

Level design shapes the game's pacing, difficulty, and flow, creating progression and challenge to keep players engaged.

Levels can enhance the narrative by creating immersive environments that reflect the story’s themes and emotions.

Great level design encourages exploration and interaction, making the game world feel alive and rewarding.

Properly designed levels ensure that the game is neither too easy nor too difficult, maintaining a balance.

Effective level design can make players feel like they are part of the game world, enhancing the overall enjoyment.

How Do I Create a Level Layout?

Define the Concept

Sketch a Map

Design the Challenges

Plan the Journey

Draw a rough top-down map to visualize the layout with key landmarks, paths, and areas of interest. It doesn't need to be detailed, just enough to outline the space.

Start with a clear idea of what you want your level to achieve. Is it a forest, a futuristic city, or a dungeon? What kind of challenges will players face?

Plan the main path and any side routes or secrets, ensuring a logical flow that guides players naturally through the environment.

Include challenges and obstacles like enemies, puzzles, platforming, or hazards that match your game's theme. Place them strategically to keep the gameplay engaging.

metadata.activity_description:
Activity:  Create an Environment of Color

Like an art project, games are designed to immerse players in their environment. Is your world warm and welcoming, or cold and tranquil? Students must open Endstar and use the tools to design a small part of their level, applying the principles of color theory.

Use AI to learn core color theory concepts and see how they apply to game design, mood setting, and player guidance.

Which AI-taught concept, complementary vs. analogous palettes, mood palettes, or the quiz format, helped you understand color theory best, and why?

If you could tweak AI’s explanation style, what would you ask for to make it even clearer?

“Explain how complementary and analogous colors work to guide player attention in a game level.”

“Suggest two color palettes (with hex codes) for a spooky forest and explain why they set the mood.”

“Quiz me on color theory with three multiple-choice questions about primary, secondary, and tertiary colors.”

Students will run one of their own color-theory prompts with AI, take notes on definitions or examples she provides, and then share which explanation style helped them most.

teacher_notes.notes:
The color wheel is a visual representation of the primary, secondary, and tertiary colors. 
The primary colors are red, blue, and yellow. When mixed, they can create secondary colors, which are orange, green, and purple. 
Tertiary colors are created by mixing a primary and secondary color together. For example, red-orange or blue-green.

The color wheel is a visual representation of the primary, secondary, and tertiary colors. 
The primary colors are red, blue, and yellow. When mixed, they can create secondary colors, which are orange, green, and purple. 
Tertiary colors are created by mixing a primary and secondary color together. For example, red-orange or blue-green.

Complementary colors are pairs of colors that are opposite each other on the color wheel.
The primary complementary colors are:
Red and green
Blue and orange
Yellow and purple
When placed next to each other, complementary colors create a strong contrast and enhance each other's intensity. They can be used to create dynamic and visually interesting designs.

Color temperature refers to how warm or cool a color is, depending on where it’s located on the color wheel. 

Warm colors, such as reds, oranges, and yellows, can be used to convey warmth, energy, and excitement. They can also be used to create a sense of intimacy or comfort. 

Cool colors, such as blues, greens, and purples, can be used to convey calmness, serenity, and stability. They can also be used to create a sense of distance or detachment.

metadata.activity_description:
Activity:  Let’s Create YOUR Mood Board

Now that you’ve learned more about mood boards, your team’s task is to create one that will inspire the design of your game. Endstar should be open as you create.You can focus on the following elements:

Other Similar Games - What games inspire your design choices

Color Palette - What types of colors do you plan to use?

Characters - What visuals have inspired your character design?

Scenery - Share images that represent the scenery and environment you envision for your game world.

Compare human vs. AI feedback on your moodboard to see how each perspective can help you refine your game’s visual vision.

Which piece of feedback, peer or AI, do you think will help your moodboard most, and why?

What AI suggestion surprised you, and how could you use it?

How will you combine both sets of comments into one clear improvement before moving on?

“Here’s my moodboard description for a [theme] platformer: [paste description]. Give me three strengths and two improvement suggestions.”

“Which of my peer’s feedback points match your suggestions, and where do you offer something new?”

“What one extra change would you make to tie my moodboard more closely to my game’s theme?”

Students will collect peer strengths/suggestions, prompt AI for her own three strengths and two improvements, then spot where feedback overlaps or differs and plan one combined revision.

✔ Include images from similar games

✔ Add a color palette, character, and scenery inspiration

✔ Upload moodboard to portfolio

✔ Create a detailed moodboard with clear design direction

✔ Explain why colors, characters, and scenery were chosen

✔ Show how the moodboard supports the game theme

✔ Design a cohesive and advanced moodboard

✔ Use color theory to strengthen visual identity

✔ Clearly link inspirations to game design decisions

Teaching Note: Have students complete the activities and then upload their progress to classkick.

teacher_notes.notes:
A mood board in video game design is a visual collage that captures the game's overall aesthetic and tone. 
It serves as inspiration and communication tool, aligning the team's vision with themes, colors, and styles through images, textures, and design elements.
Components typically include concept art, character designs, and color palettes that set the mood. 
The mood board acts as a reference throughout development, ensuring consistency and cohesion in the game's visual elements.

Color Theory + Moodboards

Level 7

Moodboard + Game Creation

Lesson 14

Lesson 14 Starter

Where Do Good Visual Ideas Come From?

What do I do?

Collect images, colours, shapes, or textures that match your game idea.

Think

What do these references have in common?
What mood or style do they suggest?

Compare (Optional)

Ask AI: “Suggest visual inspiration for a game with this mood.”
Compare AI suggestions with your own references.

Inspiration comes from observing and selecting — not copying.

AI Literacy Focus: Responsible creative use (OECD K1.4)

The Explorer's Programme

1

“AI systems learn from data written by people—and those people don’t all come from the same backgrounds, cultures, or communities.”

Ask AI - Who Trains the AI?

Fact

Relevance

Ask AI

If an AI mostly learns from one kind of voice, it might miss important ideas, values, or traditions. To make fair games and tools, we need to ask: Whose knowledge is included? Whose is left out?

Where does AI’s training data come from, and who decides what’s in it?
How could missing voices in the data affect the way AI gives advice—or the games we create?

Creating YOUR Mood Board

The Basics of Mood Boards

Lesson Objectives

What is a Moodboard?

Before you dive into creating a full game, you map out the environment, key characters, and the overall vibe. A moodboard does the same—it's the blueprint for the look and feel of your entire creative vision.

"A moodboard is like a game's starting screen."

Just like the starting screen of a game sets the tone with colors, characters, and music, a moodboard is your first step in bringing the world of your project to life. It hints at the mood and style you're aiming for, giving a sneak peek of what’s to come.

"Think of a moodboard as your game's first level map."

Why Create a Moodboard?

Visualize Concepts: Help communicate the game's aesthetic, atmosphere, and emotional impact.

Guide Design Decisions: Serve as a reference for art style, color palette, and character design.

Establish Tone: Set the mood and emotional resonance intended for players.

Align Team Vision: Ensure all team members share a common understanding of the game's artistic direction.

Activity:  Let’s Create YOUR Mood Board

Activity

Characters - What visuals have inspired your character design?
Scenery - Share images that represent the scenery and environment you envision for your game world.

What AI suggestion surprised you, and how could you use it?
How will you combine both sets of comments into one clear improvement before moving on?

“Which of my peer’s feedback points match your suggestions, and where do you offer something new?”
“What one extra change would you make to tie my moodboard more closely to my game’s theme?”

Students will collect peer strengths/suggestions, prompt AI for her own three strengths and two improvements, then spot where feedback overlaps or differs and plan one combined revision.

AI as an Assistant

✔ Include images from similar games

✔ Show how the moodboard supports the game theme

✔ Use color theory to strengthen visual identity
✔ Clearly link inspirations to game design decisions

Plenary
Game Development

A. To finalise game mechanics
B. To collect inspiration and define visual direction
C. To reduce development time
D. To replace level sketches

A. Finished code
B. Engine settings
C. Images, colours, and visual references
D. Player scores

A. The game may load slowly
B. The design may feel unoriginal or copied
C. The engine may not support visuals
D. The AI response may be short

A. Copying AI images directly

C. Selecting and adapting AI ideas alongside human inspiration
D. Letting AI define the final style

Use AI to demystify Lua—what it is, where it’s used, and why game developers choose it over other languages.

Which Lua fact from AI surprised you the most, and why?

How do Lua’s features—like being lightweight or embeddable—help make games better?

If you were making a game mod, would you choose Lua or another language, why?

“Explain what Lua is in three simple sentences for someone who’s never coded before.”

“List five popular games or apps that use Lua for scripting and give one reason each chose it.”

“Compare Lua to Python and JavaScript: why might a game developer pick Lua instead?”

Students will prompt AI to:

Explain Lua in simple terms

Identify real-world uses in games/apps

Compare its strengths to other scripting languages

Activity 1:  Basic Rule-Block Setup

Today’s activity is a basic setup using a rule-block. Students will watch the attached video, then recreate the wiring and connections. More advanced students are encouraged to design a more complex system using additional rule-blocks.

For those interested in diving deeper into this coding language, a free course is available below:

Codeacademy Lua Course HERE

All students must: Review the video guidance and accurately recreate the wired system, utilizing the same rule-blocks and props, ensuring all connections function as demonstrated.

Many students will: Modify the wired system by integrating at least one additional rule-block, enhancing the complexity while ensuring the system still functions correctly.

Some students may: Design and implement a more advanced system that incorporates two or more rule-blocks and additional props, showcasing layered functionality and interconnected components to create a dynamic and versatile setup.

The Explorer's Programme

“AI can help detect cyber attacks faster than humans—but it can also be used by hackers to find weaknesses in systems.”

Ask AI - AI & Cybersecurity

Fact

Relevance

Ask AI

Games and apps need protection from cheating, hacking, and data theft. Learning how AI strengthens or threatens cybersecurity helps you build safer, more fair digital experiences.

How is AI used to catch cheaters or hackers in online games?
Could AI ever be tricked into helping someone hack a system?

Basics of Lua

How to use Lua in Endstar

Lesson Objectives

What is Lua Scripting?

Lua is a lightweight, high-performance programming language designed for embedding into applications.
Known for its simplicity and speed, Lua is widely used in game development, scripting, and more.

Fast and Efficient: Lua is designed to be easy on your system resources.
 Flexible: Used by industry giants like Roblox, Angry Birds, and Adobe Lightroom!
 Easy to Learn: Lua’s syntax is simple, making it great for beginners and pros alike.

Why use Lua?

Lua was founded in Brazil in 1993. Lua means "moon" in Portuguese, fitting its role as a language that helps programs run smoothly behind the scenes.

Overview of Lua

Lightweight and Fast: Lua is small and fast, ideal for embedding in systems without using many resources.
Great for Embedding: Lua is built for embedding in apps, unlike languages like C++ or Java meant for standalone use.
Simple Syntax: Lua’s easy syntax is beginner-friendly, while languages like Java are more complex.
Dynamic Typing: Lua allows flexible variable types, unlike statically-typed languages like Java.
Automatic Memory Management: Lua handles memory automatically, whereas C++ requires manual management.
Game Development Focus: Lua shines in game engines, while Python and JavaScript are more general-purpose.

How does Lua compare to other coding languages?

How/Where is Lua used?

Roblox’s development team created a custom version of Lua called Luau (Lua + Roblox).

Lua scripts were essential in Far Cry for controlling enemy AI i.e. how enemies reacted to the player, communicated, and navigated the environment.

Some of the most popular addons in WoW are powered by Lua, such as Deadly Boss Mods (DBM).

What is Lua used for in Endstar?

In Endstar, Lua is utilized for user-generated scripting through the 'Scripting' feature. This is essential for enabling:

Game Modding: In Endstar, users create Lua scripts to add or edit new game mechanics or props. This allows for extensive customization of the gaming experience.
Unique Abilities: Lua scripts in Endstar will allow creators to define new character abilities or interactions, without needing to modify the underlying SDK directly.
Feature Tweaks: Users could script changes in how the game physics, AI behavior, or user interface work, tailoring the SDK to fit their exact gameplay needs.

Lua Example

Function definition

OUTPUT

Function call

This example could be used in a game or program where you need to add values and display the result. For instance:
You might use a similar script to calculate scores in a game.
It could be part of a system that adds up points or health in the background while the player plays the game.

Where and Why Would This Be Used?

What is a Rule-Block in Endstar?

In Endstar, rule-blocks visually represent a ‘block’ of code. Rule-blocks are useful for:

Visual Programming: Allow users to visually code and wire game elements together, making it easier to understand and build complex systems without needing traditional coding knowledge.
Dynamic Interactions: Users can create dynamic, real-time interactions between game objects, such as triggering events, modifying behaviors, or controlling animations.
Rapid Prototyping: Enables quick experimentation and adjustments to gameplay mechanics, allowing users to test and refine their ideas rapidly.

Gaining a deeper understanding of specific areas of theory

Finalizing any unfinished gameplay elements

As a team, you should carefully plan during this hour. There is no criteria for this activity.

Which tasks directly impact gameplay, our pitch, or understanding of objectives?

Prioritize these first (e.g., level gameplay finalization, implementing feedback).

Delegate Within Your Team:

Can everyone on the team complete different tasks today?

Assign essential tasks to each members of your team.

What small steps can I take to move our game forward?

Focus on completing one small, manageable piece at a time.

Are there tasks that can be done in 5-10 minutes?

Knock these out to reduce your to-do list and feel accomplished.

metadata.activity_description:
Students must pair up with a member from another team who is not a friend to conduct serious peer testing of their game. This process is intended to provide valuable feedback that can be used to enhance your game.

In your workbook, you will find specific headings under which to conduct the testing, as well as an area to record your observations and feedback.

Use AI to turn rough peer-testing notes into clear, rubric-aligned feedback, then compare AI-crafted comments with human insights to deepen your critique skills.

In what ways did AI’s rewritten feedback feel clearer or more helpful than your original notes?

Which suggestion—from your peer or from AI—will you act on first, and why?

How can you blend both human and AI insights to make your own feedback even stronger next time?

“Here are my raw notes on Team X’s game under Usability, Functionality, Balance, and Narrative: [paste notes]. Rewrite them into three strengths and two improvement suggestions aligned to our rubric.”

“Summarize all our feedback into a brief report for the teacher, highlighting key insights and how they map to the lesson’s learning outcomes.”

Students will prompt AI to refine their raw observations into structured feedback, use AI to articulate reflective answers, and then spot how AI’s perspective complements or differs from their classmates’.

✔ Test another team’s game

✔ Give clear feedback on usability and functionality

✔ Identify obvious bugs or issues

✔ Record feedback in the workbook

✔ Give detailed feedback on game balance or narrative

✔ Suggest specific improvements

✔ Explain how issues affect the player experience

✔ Provide comprehensive feedback across multiple areas

✔ Evaluate usability, challenge, balance, and engagement

✔ Suggest innovative improvements or extensions

Peer-Testing and Feedback

Level 9

Conducting Peer Testing for Insights

Lesson 17

Student Feedback Checkpoint

Your Voice Matters in the Explorer’s Programme
Every student must share their insights before proceeding with this lesson.

Bi-Weekly Student Feedback Form

Scan or Click

Lesson 17 Starter

Does Your Game Work for Someone Else?

What do I do?

Play another team's game.

Think

What was easy to understand?

What was confusing or frustrating?

Record

Write down one strength and one clear improvement.

Good feedback helps games improve. Honesty matters more than kindness.

Game Design Focus: Playtesting & feedback (OECD K1.3)

The Explorer's Programme

“Many jobs of the future will combine human judgement with AI support to make stronger, faster, and more balanced decisions.”

Ask AI - Future of Work

Fact

Relevance

Ask AI

By practising how to blend peer feedback with AI’s input, students are developing skills they’ll need in workplaces where people and AI collaborate daily.

How can learning to balance AI suggestions with human feedback help you in a future job?
What challenges might you face if you only trusted AI feedback without a human perspective?

Understand what peer testing is

Analyse each other’s games by peer testing them

Lesson Objectives

Step 1

What is peer testing and why is it needed?

Peer testing is a process where you test another person's game. In game development, this practice is essential as it offers critical insights into how players interact with and perceive the game.

Detailed, actionable feedback is invaluable as it allows the game creator to make specific adjustments that enhance the gaming experience

Usability: This area assesses how intuitive and user-friendly the game interface is.


Functionality: This involves checking the game for bugs, glitches, and technical issues that could disrupt gameplay. 


Gameplay Balance: This area evaluates the fairness, challenge level, and progression system of the game.


Narrative and Engagement: Testers examine how engaging the game's story and characters are.

What is involved in peer testing?

As a game maker, what should I expect from someone who is going to peer test my game, and what is expected of me when I am peer testing games for others?

metadata.activity_description:
Activity 1:  Putting Feedback in Place

Students should use this class to carefully evaluate the feedback they received on their game. By following the evaluation steps, ensure that the feedback you implement leads to meaningful improvements in gameplay.

Remember to prioritize the most important changes first and continually test your game to assess the impact of these adjustments

Use AI to organize and prioritize peer feedback—sorting comments into categories, spotting high-impact suggestions, and reflecting on your choices.

Which themes or patterns did AI spot that you hadn’t noticed, and how did that change your view of the feedback?

How did AI’s impact vs. feasibility advice help you decide what to tackle first in your game?

When drafting your reflection with AI, what made your explanation stronger or easier to understand?

“Here is my raw feedback on our game: [paste comments]. Please sort each point into usability, functionality, balance, or narrative and highlight any recurring themes.”

“From those categories, which two suggestions would have the biggest impact and are realistic for us to implement before our next test? Explain why.”

“Help me write a brief answer to: ‘What feedback did I choose to implement and why?’ in clear, student-friendly language.”

Students will work with AI to turn their scattered play-test notes into an organized plan: they’ll categorize feedback into core areas, identify the most impactful and feasible changes to make before the next prototype, and craft a clear reflection on why they chose those improvements—all in one cohesive process.

✔ Implement feedback to fix obvious issues

✔ Improve basic usability or functionality

✔ Highlight three key changes in the workbook

✔ Apply feedback to improve game balance or narrative

✔ Refine progression or story elements

✔ Highlight three key improvements clearly

✔ Make thoughtful innovations based on feedback

✔ Improve usability, balance, and narrative together

✔ Highlight more than three key changes

Teaching Note: Have students complete the activities and then upload their progress to classkick.

Peer-Testing and Feedback

Level 9

Incorporating Feedback into Game Design

Lesson 18

Lesson 18 Starter

What Should We Fix First?

What do I do?

Review the feedback your game received.

Think

Which issues affect the player experience the most?

Which ones can realistically be fixed?

Decide

Choose one improvement to focus on today.

Iteration is about making smart improvements — not fixing everything.

Game Design Focus: Iteration & improvement (OECD K1.1)

The Explorer's Programme

“AI is regulated differently around the world, with governments setting priorities for safety, fairness, and innovation.”

Ask AI - AI Regulation

Fact

Relevance

Ask AI

Just like governments set different priorities when regulating AI, you have to make choices about what matters most when improving your game. Thinking about how others weigh safety, fairness, and progress might help you question your own priorities: Are you protecting the player experience? Are you pushing innovation? How would you balance those trade-offs if you were setting the rules?

How do you think laws in different countries might shape what improvements are seen as most important for AI development?
What could you learn from how governments balance safety with progress when you plan your game updates?

Analyse and implement the received feedback into your game

Lesson Objectives

Step 1

Why should I use the feedback I received?

Peer feedback provides real-time insights from individuals with similar learning experiences, allowing for more relevant and targeted suggestions.

By using this feedback, you can make sure your work is stronger, and it also teaches you how to listen and improve based on suggestions from others. This is a great skill for both learning and working with others.

Organize Feedback: Categorize comments into usability, functionality, gameplay balance, etc., to focus on key areas.

Identify Patterns: Look for recurring feedback to highlight priority issues that affect gameplay.

Assess Feasibility: Decide what can realistically be changed based on resources and alignment with your game’s vision.

Prioritize Changes: Focus on fixes with the highest impact, like bugs or critical usability issues.

Test & Iterate: Implement changes, test, and seek further feedback to ensure improvement.

How do I evaluate the feedback I receive?

Evaluating feedback can be challenging, especially when you are personally invested in your ideas. However, it’s crucial to approach feedback with an open mind and assess it objectively. To help you, here are some useful tips:

metadata.activity_description:
Activity 1:  Time to Validate

Students must team up and work on validating market demand for their own product ideas (or use pre-selected ideas from the teacher). Each team will:

Step 1: Design a simple survey or interview script.

Step 2: Conduct 5-10 interviews or survey 20+ potential customers.

Step 3: Compile and analyze the results, identifying key trends or insights about market demand.

Use AI to create clear, unbiased survey questions, grasp what product–market fit means in games, and turn real feedback into honest insights.

How did AI’s survey questions help you get more honest feedback, and what biased question did you avoid?

What new insight did AI’s product–market fit explanation give you about whether players really want your game?

Which demand pattern from the analysis surprised you most, and how will it shape your next design step?

“Based on our platformer’s theme and audience, write five neutral, inclusive survey questions that avoid leading language.”

“What is product–market fit in gaming, and how can I tell if my game has achieved it?”

“Here are our survey responses: [paste data]. Identify the top two demand patterns and flag any feedback that might be biased or one-off.”

Students will work with AI to design a simple, ethical survey or interview script tailored to their game concept, ask AI to explain product–market fit in gaming and how to recognize it, then feed AI actual survey or interview responses to spot key demand patterns and avoid bias.

✔ Complete a basic validation activity (survey or interviews)

✔ Show clear interest or lack of interest in the idea

✔ Analyse customer feedback in detail

✔ Identify clear signs of demand

✔ Highlight trends or patterns

✔ Present strong validation evidence

✔ Use engagement data, sign-ups, or pre-orders

✔ Clearly demonstrate market demand
