slides.content:
Validating Product Market Fit

Level 10

Validate and Demonstrate Market Demand

Lesson 19

Lesson 19 Starter

Does Your Game Fit Its Players?

What do I do?

Look at the feedback, interviews, or tester comments you have received so far.

Think

What patterns or trends do you notice?

What do players enjoy or struggle with?

Decide

Does your game match its intended audience?

Data helps inform decisions — it does not make them for you.

AI Literacy Focus: Analysing reliability & ethical responsibility (OECD K1.4)

The Explorer's Programme

“Even when AI gives you a clear answer, it might still be wrong—especially if it makes up facts or misinterprets the question.”

Ask AI - Fact-Check Mode

Fact

Relevance

Ask AI

In game design, research, or any creative project, trusting the wrong info can break your story, your code, or your pitch. Learning how to double-check what AI tells you helps you stay sharp and accurate.

What are three quick ways I can fact-check AI’s answers?
How do I know if a confident answer might be based on poor or missing sources?

What market demand is and why it matters.

How to validate demand before launching a product.

How to demonstrate that a market exists for your product.

Lesson Objectives

Step 1

What is Market Demand and Why is it Important?

Market demand in the game-making industry means how many people want to buy or play a game at a certain price. 

By knowing what players want, game developers can create more popular games and make better choices for their business.

Question: Can you think of a game that experienced a huge surge in market demand?

Research:
What It Involves: Studying trends, analyzing competitors, and identifying customer needs.
Why It’s Important: Helps you understand current gaps in the market and how your game fits in. Competitor analysis reveals strengths and weaknesses in similar products.

Data Analysis:
What It Involves: Using tools like surveys, pre-orders, and market reports to quantify interest in your game.
Why It’s Important: Provides concrete numbers to back up your assumptions. Data helps you refine your game and ensure there's a demand before investing heavily in development.

Customer Feedback:
What It Involves: Engaging directly with your target audience through interviews, focus groups, or online communities to validate your game idea.
Why It’s Important: Real-time feedback allows you to adapt and improve your game before launch. It also provides insights into features your audience values the most.

Step 1: Understanding Market Demand

Surveys and Interviews: Engage potential customers to get direct feedback on their interest in your product.

Pre-orders and Waitlists: Encourage customers to commit early through pre-orders or sign-up forms.

Social Media and Online Communities: Share your idea and track how much interest it generates.

Market Research Tools: Use data analytics tools to assess potential demand based on market trends.

Step 2: Validating Market Demand

There are several methods to validate market demand, here are some examples:

1. Presenting data from surveys or pre-orders. 

Sample: Create a short, interactive survey using a tool like Google Forms or Typeform that includes images or video clips of your product. Share it in school groups or youth forums to gather feedback.

2. Showing evidence of social media buzz or high engagement levels in your target audience.

Sample: Run a social media challenge or poll on platforms like Instagram or TikTok, and measure how many people participate or engage. Track likes, comments, and shares to showcase interest.

3. Highlighting competitor success or market gaps that your product fills.

Sample: Create a fun visual comparison chart or video (using Canva or iMovie) that highlights where your product stands out from the competition. Present it to your class for feedback.

Step 3: Demonstrating Market Demand

Activity 1:  Time to Validate

Students must team up and work on validating market demand for their own product ideas (or use pre-selected ideas from the teacher). Each team will:

Step 1: Design a simple survey or interview script.
Step 2: Conduct 5-10 interviews or survey 20+ potential customers.
Step 3: Compile and analyze the results, identifying key trends or insights about market demand.

Activity

Portfolio Evidence — Lesson 19
What you built + how you used AI

Use AI to create clear, unbiased survey questions, grasp what product–market fit means in games, and turn real feedback into honest insights.

How did AI’s survey questions help you get more honest feedback, and what biased question did you avoid?
What new insight did AI’s product–market fit explanation give you about whether players really want your game?
Which demand pattern from the analysis surprised you most, and how will it shape your next design step?

“Based on our platformer’s theme and audience, write five neutral, inclusive survey questions that avoid leading language.”
“What is product–market fit in gaming, and how can I tell if my game has achieved it?”
“Here are our survey responses: [paste data]. Identify the top two demand patterns and flag any feedback that might be biased or one-off.”

Students will work with AI to design a simple, ethical survey or interview script tailored to their game concept, ask AI to explain product–market fit in gaming and how to recognize it, then feed AI actual survey or interview responses to spot key demand patterns and avoid bias.

AI as an Assistant

All students must:
✔ Complete a basic validation activity (survey or interviews)
✔ Show clear interest or lack of interest in the idea
✔ Record results clearly

Many students will: 
✔ Analyse customer feedback in detail
✔ Identify clear signs of demand
✔ Highlight trends or patterns

Some students may: 
✔ Present strong validation evidence
✔ Use engagement data, sign-ups, or pre-orders
✔ Clearly demonstrate market demand

Criteria

Assessment For Learning

Plenary
Game Development
GD-19.1
What evidence best helps validate Product–Market Fit?
A. Developer opinions and meetings
B. Player feedback and survey results
C. Visual quality and color theory
D. Number of features and mechanics
GD-19.2
Which result suggests good Product–Market Fit?
A. Players finish the game quickly
B. Players understand and enjoy the game as intended
C. The game uses advanced mechanics
D. The game looks professional

Assessment For Learning

Plenary
AI Literacy (OECD K1.4)
AI-19.1
Why should students be cautious when using AI to analyse survey results?
A. AI avoids statistics and is always biased
B. AI may overlook context and nuance in responses
C. AI prefers positive feedback
D. AI cannot read charts
AI-19.2
What is the student’s responsibility after AI highlights trends in data?
A. Accept the results as final
B. Remove conflicting feedback
C. Interpret the trends and decide what they mean for the game
D. Ignore outliers automatically

| Customer Feedback | Feedback gathered directly from target audience, detailed and relevant. | Feedback from some customers but lacking depth. | Some feedback gathered, with general comments. | Little feedback gathered, not very useful for validation. | No relevant customer feedback collected. |
| Demonstration of Demand | Clear evidence of market demand through data and insights. | Some evidence of demand but lacks strong data points. | Limited evidence of demand, but some interest shown. | Weak demonstration of demand, unclear conclusions. | No evidence of demand shown. |

Ask AI - Project Leadership & Collaboration

By practising timeboxing and role assignments with AI, students are learning how real project leaders break down complex tasks, motivate teammates, and guide groups through challenges — all skills that matter far beyond the classroom.

What makes someone a good team leader in a time-limited project?

How could you help your team stay motivated and confident through stressful rehearsals?

Activity 1:  Create Your Presentation

Students should use a presentation tool to outline your game’s concept, market demand and features. As a Team, use the template provided with everyone having a role in the presentation. Make sure to include your problem, journey and solution.

Don’t forget to add at least 5 visuals to bring your game to life and support your data. Remember, a picture is worth a thousand words, so use visuals to make a strong impact.

Use AI to co-create a clear, time-boxed plan and assign roles so your team can draft, build, and rehearse the final pitch efficiently with the provided pitch template.

Which time block in our schedule feels most critical for success, and why?

Which practice question from AI was hardest to answer, and how can we improve our response?

“We have 3 hours to finish and practice our pitch using the Student Pitch Template. Create a schedule with three 1-hour sessions—detailing start/end times and tasks for planning, slide design, and rehearsal.”

“Based on our team’s strengths (speaker, writer, designer, timekeeper), assign roles and suggest specific tasks each person should handle in our timeline.”

“Give us three likely audience questions about our pitch and draft concise, confident responses we can practice.”

Students will work with AI to break a 3-hour pitch session into digestible blocks—planning, slide creation, rehearsal—and map out who does what. They’ll also use AI to simulate audience Q&A, ensuring they can refine clarity and tone before the final presentation.

Game Design Focus: Presentation design & storytelling (OECD K1.3)

The Explorer's Programme

“Strong project leaders make sure everyone knows their role, timelines are realistic, and the whole team feels supported to succeed.”

Ask AI - Project Leadership & Collaboration

Fact

Relevance

Ask AI

By practising timeboxing and role assignments with AI, students are learning how real project leaders break down complex tasks, motivate teammates, and guide groups through challenges — all skills that matter far beyond the classroom.

What makes someone a good team leader in a time-limited project?

The importance of structuring a presentation clearly and logically.

Creating a presentation that engages and informs the audience.

Using visuals and storytelling to enhance your presentation.

Lesson Objectives

Step 1

Why is Structure Important in a Presentation?

A well-structured presentation helps the audience follow along, keeps them engaged, and delivers the message effectively.
To structure your presentation:

Introduction: Grab your audience’s attention by introducing the main topic and why it matters.
Main Body: Break down the core content into clear, easy-to-follow sections (features, market demand, product highlights).
Conclusion: Recap the main points, offer a call to action, or suggest next steps.

The Problem: What problem does your game solve, or what experience does it offer?

The Journey: How did your team develop the game? What challenges did you face?

The Solution: Present your game as the solution to the problem or the unique experience you’re offering.

Using Storytelling to Engage Your Audience

Telling a story creates an emotional connection with your audience. When structuring your game presentation, consider:

Screenshots or Video Demos: Show gameplay footage or features.

Sample: Capture the most exciting and relevant parts of your game, such as gameplay mechanics, unique features, or character designs.

The Journey: How did you validate the market? What challenges did you face?

Sample: Create charts or graphs that display critical data such as market demand, competitor analysis, or player feedback.

The Solution: Present your game as the solution to the problem or the unique experience you’re offering.

Sample: Use infographics to visually represent complex ideas or processes, such as the development timeline of your game.

Adding Visuals to Support Your Presentation

Text vs Visual Format

Text

Visual

On Instagram, our posts and stories generated a total of 2,300 views, driven by our use of engaging visuals and interactive polls. TikTok was the standout platform with 4,500 views, where our short gameplay clips consistently went viral. On Discord, where we shared sneak peeks with gaming communities, we received 1,200 views across multiple servers. Our posts on X were less impactful, with a total of 800 views, but Reddit performed surprisingly well, where discussion threads about our game brought in 1,700 views. Finally, on BeReal, we received a modest 500 views, as we shared behind-the-scenes content of our development process. In total, our promotional campaign garnered 11,000 views across all platforms, helping us identify TikTok and Instagram as the most effective channels for driving interest in our game.

Question 1:  What are the main differences between both formats?

Question 2:  Which do you believe is more effective, and why?

Question 3: Knowing this, what will you make visual and/or text on your presentation?

Let’s Discuss

Lesson 21

Practicing and Delivering Demo Pitches

Student Feedback Checkpoint

Your Voice Matters in the Explorer’s Programme
Every student must share their insights before proceeding with this lesson.

Bi-Weekly Student Feedback Form

Scan or Click

Lesson 21 Starter

Can You Explain Your Game Clearly?

What do I do?

Practise your pitch with your team.

Think

What parts were clear?

Where did you hesitate or get confused?

Refine

Update your speaking points or slides.

Clear communication builds confidence and trust.

Game Design Focus: Pitch rehearsal & collaboration (OECD K1.3)

The Explorer's Programme

“People with a growth mindset see challenges and feedback as chances to learn and get better, instead of reasons to give up.”

Ask AI - Growth Mindset

Fact

Relevance

Ask AI

When students polish their pitch with AI and handle hard questions, they are practicing how to learn from critique, stay confident, and keep improving — all essential skills for presenting ideas and leading projects in the future.

How can you turn a tough question into a positive learning moment?
What mindset helps you stay calm and confident when someone challenges your ideas?

How to fully finalize our games

The best way to practice our showcase presentations

Lesson Objectives

Step 1

Finalizing our team’s game

Firstly, go through every part of your game carefully. Make sure the props, challenges, and how the game’s story plays out works as you intended.

It's very important to review and fix any issues now so your game is ready present.

Use AI to polish every part of your demo pitch—checking slides against your checklist, sharpening your script, and rehearsing Q&A—so you deliver a clear, confident team presentation.

Which piece of AI’s slide feedback revealed a gap you hadn’t noticed, and how will you fix it?
How did rewriting your script with AI change the way you’ll deliver your section?
Which simulated question was toughest, and what strategy will you use to answer it smoothly in the real showcase?

“Here’s the text from our slide on market fit: [paste slide]. Against our checklist, what’s missing or needs clearer wording?”
“This is my speaking script for the game-story section: [paste script]. Can you rewrite it to be more confident and engaging for a judge panel?”
“Act like a Demo Day judge and ask us three challenging questions about our game’s design and launch plan.”

Students will collaborate with AI to finalize their pitch: reviewing slides for missing checklist items, refining scripts for clarity and tone, and rehearsing with simulated audience questions. Each team member will know their part, the slides will hit all key points, and the group will be ready for tough questions at the showcase.

AI as an Assistant

What key elements should I have included in my presentation?

To ensure you’re ready to begin your presentation, please follow this checklist:

Game Story and Engagement:


Does your game have a clear and engaging story that players can follow?

Have you included interesting challenges that keep players excited?


Testing and Feedback:


Have you tested the game with someone who is not on your team?

Did you use the feedback to make your game better?

Product Market Fit:


Can you explain why people would want to play your game?

Have you shown that your game meets the needs and interests of your intended audience?

Go-To-Market Strategy:

Who are you planning to sell your game to?

What is your plan to tell these people about your game and get them excited to play it?


Presentation Preparation:

Do you know what to say about your game during the showcase?

Can you explain how you worked as a team?

Are your presentation slides clear, engaging, and ready to show?

Step 2

Are you ready to ‘Showcase’?

Once you've completed all the items on your checklist, you should be fully prepared to present at our Showcase.

Remember, creating your game was a team effort, and presenting it should be too. Work together to make your presentation as successful as your game development.

Activity

Activity 1: Demo Day Rehearsal

Using the presentation template provided, it’s now your time to effectively showcase your game. Remember, the presentation tasks should be shared among team members. 

Just like in game development, successful collaboration and delegation are key to a standout performance in the Showcase.

Assessment For Learning

Plenary
Game Development
GD-21.1
During a rehearsal, a team finishes within the time limit but judges still seem confused. What is the most important issue to address before the final pitch?
A. Adding more features to the game
B. Improving slide visuals and animations
C. Clarifying the problem, solution, and player value proposition
D. Increasing the speaking volume of the presenter
GD-21.2
Which behaviour best indicates a team is ready for a live pitch?
A. Every member memorises the script word-for-word
B. The slides explain everything without speaking
C. Team members can adapt their explanation if questioned
D. The pitch matches the AI-generated script exactly

Assessment For Learning

Plenary
AI-21.1 (OECD K1.3)
A team relies on AI to generate their pitch script. What is the greatest risk during a live presentation?
A. The pitch will sound too formal
B. The team may struggle to explain or defend ideas under questioning
C. The slides may look generic and may not contain relevant images to the game created
D. The pitch may exceed the time limit
AI - 21.2
Which situation best shows responsible use of AI when preparing a pitch?
A. Using AI to write the full script and memorising it
B. Letting AI decide which ideas are most impressive
C. Using AI to draft structure, then rewriting in the team’s own words
D. Asking AI to predict judge questions

metadata.activity_description:
Use Ana as a “critical friend” to review your full Showcase presentation—checking clarity, flow, and checklist coverage—then refine slides, script, and talking order to maximize audience impact.

Which of Ana’s suggestions uncovered a gap you hadn’t spotted, and how will you fix it?

How did adjusting the flow or speaking order change your confidence in delivering the pitch?

What final tweak from Ana—on visuals or wording—will you add to make your presentation stand out?

“Ana, here’s our slide outline and speaking script for the Showcase. What’s missing or unclear based on our presentation checklist?””

“Ana, does this sequence flow well? Please tell us if it’s clear who speaks when, and suggest any adjustments to improve pacing or transitions.”

“Ana, make our conclusion slide more powerful and memorable—then rewrite our closing script to match that tone.”

Students will collaborate with Ana to perform a final, end-to-end review of their pitch: they’ll have Ana compare their slide outline and script against the lesson’s presentation checklist, suggest improvements for engagement and coherence, and simulate audience Q&A—ensuring each team member knows their part and the pitch meets all success criteria before delivery.

Activity 1:  Finalise your Presentation

Students have had a chance to start their presentations and today is aimed at finalising these. Your task is to carefully follow the template provided and make any necessary additions to ensure your presentation is engaging, clear, and ready to deliver.

Just like in game development, successful collaboration and delegation are key to a standout performance in the Showcase.

✔ Rehearse the full pitch as a team

✔ Clearly explain the game’s purpose and mechanics

✔ Stay within the presentation structure provided

✔ Refine explanations based on rehearsal feedback

✔ Improve clarity, timing, and role coordination

✔ Highlight unique or innovative elements confidently

✔ Deliver a confident, polished rehearsal pitch

✔ Integrate live gameplay or interactive moments

✔ Adjust presentation flow to better engage the audience

Lesson 22

Lesson 22 Starter

How Do You Respond to Questions?

What do I do?

Answer questions about your game from another group or your teacher, demonstrating your understanding and communication skills.

Think

Did the questions reveal gaps in your explanation or game design?

Did your answers clearly convey what you intended, or was there misunderstanding?

Adjust

Improve your responses, refine your pitch, or update your slides based on the feedback received.

Questions help strengthen your ideas by revealing areas for clarity and improvement.

Game Design Focus: Reflection & communication under pressure (OECD K1.3)

The Explorer's Programme

“Persuasive communication combines logic, emotion, and credibility to move an audience toward your point of view.”

Ask AI - Persuasive Communication

Fact

Relevance

Ask AI

As you rehearse and adjust your final pitch with Ana, you’re practicing persuasive skills — not just what you say, but how you say it to inspire confidence and action. These skills will serve you in pitches, job interviews, and real-world leadership roles.

What makes a closing statement powerful and memorable?
How can you adjust tone or pacing to better persuade your audience?

Review your presentation checklist

Start creating your ‘Showcase’ presentation
